text: The surgeon identifies the ulcer at the gastrojejunal anastomosis with minimal spillage. The omentum is pulled up and checked for tension-free coverage. The ulcer is closed with a permanent suture placed proximal to it, which is then passed through the omentum. The knot is tied, bringing the omentum over the ulcer as a patch. An endoscopic biopsy of the ulcer is performed through the mouth.
The video shows a patient with a perforated pre-pyloric ulcer, identified by CT scan, presenting with significant inflammation around the ulcer site.

The surgeon identifies the ulcer and accesses the remnant stomach using Keith needles to secure the gastric wall. Electrocautery is used to create a gastrostomy, and a 12mm trocar is placed into the stomach. A diagnostic endoscope is introduced for evaluation and biopsy of the ulcer. The gastrostomy is closed with a stapler, and an omental patch is applied to repair the ulcer.
The small bowel is insufflated, which complicates visualization during the procedure. No bowel clamp is applied, affecting the clarity of the surgical field.

The surgeon demonstrates a perforated prepyloic ulcer in a patient with a retrocolic-retrogastric bypass. The omentum covers the ulcer, which has perforated into the falciform ligament and liver, forming a liver abscess. Stay sutures secure the stomach against the anterior abdominal wall, facilitated by the retrocholic rulem. A gastrostomy is created using electrocautery, and a 12mm trocar is inserted into the stomach. A bowel clamp is applied to the biliopancreatic limb to prevent insufflation of the small bowel during endoscopy. Endoscopic examination and biopsies of the remnant stomach are performed, followed by closure of the gastrostomy with a stapler. The ulcer is repaired using an omental patch.
